misconceptionSummary: Students use a digit that does not exist in the base — writing a 2 or 3 in a binary number.

lines.text: Digit '2' does not exist in base 2

steps.narration: The student correctly found that four plus two equals six, but wrote the column values as digits. In base two, the only allowed digits are zero and one — two is not a valid binary digit.

visuals.ruleText: In base n, every digit must be less than n — binary allows only 0 and 1
visuals.subText: A digit of 2 in base 2 would overflow into the next column

steps.narration: Each digit represents how many of that column you have — and you can never have as many as the base itself, because that would overflow into the next column. Binary digits are always zero or one.

lines.text: 6 in binary: which powers of 2 sum to 6?

steps.narration: Place a one in each column whose value is used and a zero in each that is not. Four and two are used; one is not. The binary digits are one, one, zero — reading from the largest column.

visuals.label: Use 0s and 1s only — 1 if column is included, 0 if not

steps.narration: Six in base ten is one one zero in base two. Each position records whether that power of two contributes — one if yes, zero if no.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 24₂ for six — using the digit 2 in binary. Binary only has 0 and 1. Write a 1 for each power of 2 that is included and a 0 for each that is not.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: No digit in a number system can equal or exceed the base. Binary uses only 0 and 1 — never 2 or higher. Place 1s and 0s to show which powers of 2 are included.
